content_preview.title: Neural Data Privacy Bills Sweep Across US States as Consumer Brain-Reading Devices Outpace Federal Regulation
content_preview.summary: At least nine bills across six states have been introduced in 2026 to regulate the collection of brain and nervous system data, as consumer neurotechnology devices proliferate and the federal MIND Act awaits action in Congress.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## A Wave of State Legislation

In the first weeks of 2026, lawmakers in at least six US states introduced nine bills aimed at regulating how companies collect, store, and sell data generated by measuring brain and nervous system activity. Alabama, California, Illinois, New York, Vermont, and Virginia have all put forward proposals that would impose consent requirements, purpose limitations, and deletion rights on what regulators increasingly call "neural data" -- information that, unlike conven...

editor_notes.content_quality: Excellent policy article with detailed state-by-state analysis, federal context, and industry data.
editor_notes.source_verification: CSIS: verified — $38B market, neural data sensitivity, HIPAA gap, FTC authority untested. Congress.gov: 403 bot-blocked but is official US government source, MIND Act details corroborated by CSIS. EFF: verified — 30 consumer devices, Muse 100M hours, Neurorights Foundation 29/30 companies, thought-to-text accuracy figures.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All verifiable claims confirmed. Specific state bill numbers and provisions could not be independently verified via these sources but are plausible given the legislative context.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and analytical. Presents regulatory landscape without advocacy.
editor_notes.originality: Fresh topic — no prior neural data privacy coverage.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Outstanding digital rights article with strong policy analysis. Approved.
